summary: Ronacher compares Mako, Jinja, and Genshi as second-generation Python template engines with distinct philosophies and tradeoffs. He explains how his own needs, including WSGI routing, dynamic inheritance, and performance, led him to use Mako for logic-heavy templates. He contrasts that with Jinja’s designer-friendly, Django-inspired syntax and safer, more expressive template language. He then positions Genshi as a powerful XML-stream engine with automatic escaping and transformation features but a steeper learning curve and slower performance. He concludes that the variety is a strength and that different contexts justify different engines.
key_points: All three engines are modern, framework-agnostic, Unicode-capable successors to earlier template tools, but each targets a different workflow.
Mako emphasizes Pythonic logic, dynamic inheritance, and performance, making it ideal when templates need real code.
Jinja keeps a Django-like, sandboxed syntax but removes restrictive expression limits to reduce template tag boilerplate.
Genshi’s XML-stream model enables context-aware escaping and powerful transforms, at the cost of speed and designer accessibility.
Tool choice should follow project context, not a single universal preference.
money_quotes: All three template engines are totally different but have a one thing in common: All three are the "second generation" of template engines.
Mako is a great template engine if you know Python, if you need some logic in templates (and you know: logic in templates is not bad in every situation) and if you need the best performance.
When Mako is like PHP, Jinja is like Smarty (even though Mako is a million times better than PHP as template engine).
This lead to the development of what is now known as Jinja.
Because it's stream based you can rewrite streams during the rendering process.
And lastly: this whole stream processing makes Genshi slow.
I hope this post sums up why I'm using all three template engines and why I think we should be happy that we can chose between a couple of template engines :-)
key_insight: Template engines embody different tradeoffs, so choosing among Mako, Jinja, and Genshi should be driven by the project’s workflow, audience, and performance needs.
